title: TPU v1 · Formal verification: proving deadlock freedom and handshake invariants within a constrained model
oneLine: Host-command FSMs, PCIe and DDR3 handshakes, and cross-domain control contain rare protocol corners that simulation cannot exhaust. Formal verification gives properties to an engine that explores every reachable state: it either proves them invariant (PROVEN) or returns a counterexample waveform. The 256×256 array datapath creates state-space explosion, so proof scope must be defined carefully.

(illustrative)</text></g><g class="cs-part" data-k="dut" data-g="dut"><rect class="cs-box" x="40" y="170" width="230" height="74" rx="8"/><text class="cs-t" x="155" y="196">RTL under proof</text><text class="cs-sub" x="155" y="216">Instruction FSM / PCIe·DDR3 handshake</text><text class="cs-sub" x="155" y="233">Scope excludes the 256×256 array</text></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="270" y1="97" x2="332" y2="120" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><line class="cs-arr" x1="270" y1="207" x2="332" y2="160" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><g class="cs-part" data-k="fpv" data-g="fpv"><rect class="cs-box" x="336" y="110" width="180" height="80" rx="8"/><text class="cs-t" x="426" y="140">Formal engine</text><text class="cs-sub" x="426" y="160">Explore all reachable states in scope</text><text class="cs-sub" x="426" y="177">Mathematical proof, not trace simulation</text></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="516" y1="138" x2="582" y2="100" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><line class="cs-arr" x1="516" y1="162" x2="582" y2="300" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><g class="cs-part" data-k="proven" data-g="proven"><rect class="cs-box" x="586" y="66" width="120" height="64" rx="8"/><text class="cs-t" x="646" y="92">PROVEN</text><text class="cs-sub" x="646" y="112">Property invariant</text></g><g class="cs-part" data-k="cex" data-g="cex"><rect class="cs-box" x="586" y="270" width="120" height="64" rx="8"/><text class="cs-t" x="646" y="296">Counterexample CEX</text><text class="cs-sub" x="646" y="316">One violating waveform</text></g><g class="cs-part" data-k="sim" data-g="sim"><rect class="cs-box" x="40" y="300" width="476" height="68" rx="8"/><text class="cs-t" x="278" y="326">Contrast: simulation traverses only finite stimulus paths</text><text class="cs-sub" x="278" y="346">Deep corners / rare state combinations may never occur → deadlock escapes</text></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="278" y1="244" x2="278" y2="296" stroke-dasharray="5 4" marker-end="url(#csar)"/></svg>

legend.name: Property under proof (SVA assertion)
legend.desc: Encode the guarantee in SystemVerilog Assertion: for example, once req occurs, gnt arrives within N cycles (no deadlock); valid/ready never lock up; or the FSM never enters an undefined state. The formal engine proves that property across all reachable states. assert(req |-> ##[1:N] gnt); is illustrative, not TPU disclosure.

legend.name: RTL under proof (bounded scope)
legend.desc: Target control-intensive, narrow-data logic: host CISC decode/scheduling FSMs, PCIe handshakes, DDR3-controller handshakes, and CDC control. Scope is critical—never submit the full 256×256 array, 65,536 cells, unchanged, or exponential state growth prevents closure.

legend.name: Formal engine (exhaustive within scope)
legend.desc: Unlike simulation, which evaluates one supplied trace at a time, formal uses reachability, induction, and model checking to cover all reachable states in the scoped logic. For finite but corner-rich protocols and FSMs, it can guarantee behavior for every input sequence within assumptions.

legend.name: PROVEN result (invariant property)
legend.desc: If the engine explores the reachable state space without a violation, it returns PROVEN: the property holds throughout the stated scope. The guarantee applies only to logic inside the proof boundary and to correctly specified environmental assumptions.

legend.name: Counterexample (CEX)
legend.desc: If any input sequence violates the property, the engine returns a concrete waveform from reset to failure. Engineers can replay the complete signal trace to reproduce and localize the bug—one of formal verification's most valuable outputs.

legend.name: Contrast: simulation traverses finite paths
legend.desc: Simulation covers only directed or randomly generated traces. A rare combination—such as FIFO nearly full during reset while two handshakes transition—may never occur and a deadlock can escape. Formal fills this gap without requiring engineers to imagine the trigger.

steps.cap: ① Select control-intensive targets: instruction FSMs, PCIe/DDR3 handshakes, and CDC control. Express deadlock freedom, protocol invariants, and no illegal FSM state as SVA, explicitly excluding the large array datapath.
② Give the scoped RTL, properties, and environmental constraints to the formal engine. It explores every reachable state mathematically rather than executing stimulus traces one by one.
③ If all reachable states satisfy the property → PROVEN. This is materially stronger than “ten thousand simulations did not fail” within the declared scope.
④ If a violation exists → counterexample waveform from reset through failure, directly reproducible for debugging.
⑤ Simulation covers finite stimulus paths and can miss deep deadlocks. Formal's value is independence from whether someone happened to write the triggering test.

before: Select formal-suitable logic and define scope: formal excels on control-dense, state-rich, narrow-data blocks such as TPU's host instruction FSM, PCIe/DDR3 interface handshakes, and CDC controls. A 256×256 array and Unified Buffer create exponential state growth, so exclude, abstract, or partition them before beginning.

Translate guarantees into provable assertions: formalize goals rather than “run and inspect”: every FSM progresses within finite time, every req eventually receives gnt, valid/ready cannot deadlock, no illegal state is reached, and counters remain in bounds. These SVA properties are the completion criteria.

Constrain the environment with assume to prevent false counterexamples: formal explores physically impossible inputs unless restricted. Encode protocol legality—for example, req cannot be withdrawn prematurely—so counterexamples remain meaningful. Assumptions must match interface specifications.

Separate formal from simulation responsibilities: within a bounded, correctly constrained proof scope, formal can exhaustively establish control and protocol properties; simulation and coverage validate broader datapaths and end-to-end function. Formal closes on PROVEN/CEX or an explicitly documented inconclusive result, while simulation closes on coverage and bug convergence. Assign every concern to avoid duplication or gaps.
during: Start with small properties and narrow scope, then expand: prove one or two essential properties for one FSM or handshake and confirm timely PROVEN or CEX before adding properties and boundaries. A large block with dozens of properties can explode before yielding a conclusion.

Rule: every control FSM needs deadlock-freedom/liveness proof: deadlock under a rare state combination is easy to miss in simulation and difficult to debug in silicon. Prove liveness—every req eventually receives a response and every state can eventually exit—for TPU-like multi-interface, multi-clock control.

Treat every counterexample seriously: determine whether a CEX exposes a real RTL defect or a missing environmental assumption. Fix real logic; add only protocol-valid assumptions for false traces and prove again. Never modify RTL for an impossible trace or overconstrain away a real bug.

Abstract and partition proofs that do not converge: timeout/inconclusive is neither PROVEN nor CEX. Abstract wide data buses to arbitrary values while retaining controls, cut proof boundaries, or add helper invariants to reduce the problem to a closable state space.

Trade-off: formal complements rather than replaces simulation: formal guarantees all inputs within a scoped, assumed model, while simulation covers broader functionality but not every corner. Use formal for handshake/FSM deadlock and coverage-driven simulation for array datapath behavior.

Scope note: TPU's formal environment is not public: command FSMs, PCIe/DDR3 handshakes, and multiple clock domains follow from TPU v1 architecture, but tools, exact properties, and proof runtime are undisclosed engineering details. The workflow here is standard control-ASIC formal practice.

result.label: Best-suited targets
result.value: Control FSMs · PCIe/DDR3 handshakes · CDC control paths

result.label: Primary properties
result.value: Deadlock freedom (liveness) · handshake invariants · no illegal state

result.label: Environment constraint example
result.value: assume property(protocol-legal input); prevents false counterexamples (illustrative)

result.label: Scope boundary
result.value: Exclude the 256×256 array datapath to prevent state explosion

after: Confirm every property reaches PROVEN: audit deadlock, liveness, and safety properties for each FSM and handshake. Do not silently treat timeout/inconclusive as success; abstract further or record an explicitly partial proof.

Re-prove after every counterexample fix: rerun the full formal property set after correcting RTL so the failed property becomes PROVEN and no new violation appears. Formal regression runs continuously alongside simulation regression.

Audit assumptions to prevent false PROVEN: overly strong assume statements can exclude violating behavior and prove an empty world. Review constraints, cover assumed behavior, and inspect reachability to ensure the proof is not overconstrained.

Combine formal results with simulation coverage for signoff: formal control/protocol proofs + simulation datapath coverage jointly establish functional verification. Only when formal is fully PROVEN and simulation coverage closes may the design proceed to synthesis (⑦).

Document the evidence boundary: distinguish TPU v1's public structural facts—command FSM, PCIe/DDR3 interfaces, and multiple domains—from standard methodology such as SVA, assume constraints, abstraction, and partitioning.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (public structures include host CISC commands over PCIe, off-chip DDR3, and a 700 MHz core array; exact formal tools, properties, and environments are standard-practice examples because TPU has not published its formal methodology)
